## ASSISTANT

Rust workspace = one `target/` dir, one `Cargo.lock`, many crates. Closest TS analog is a monorepo with project references.

**Compilation unit**: In TS, each `tsconfig.json` with `composite: true` is a project that compiles independently and produces `.d.ts` declarations for downstream consumers. In Rust, each crate compiles independently and produces `.rlib` (metadata + code) for downstream consumers. Same idea.

**Dependency graph**: TS project references are explicit in `tsconfig.json` `references` array. Rust infers the graph from `Cargo.toml` `[dependencies]` with `path = "../other"`. No manual reference wiring.

**Incremental**: TS uses `.tsbuildinfo` files to skip unchanged projects. Rust uses fingerprints in `target/` to skip unchanged crates. Both only recompile what changed and everything downstream of it.

**Key difference -- linking**: TS projects produce independent `.js` output. Rust crates all link into a single binary at the end. That final link step touches every crate's object code. TS has no equivalent cost.

**Key difference -- generics**: TS erases types at compile time. Generics have zero compile cost. Rust monomorphizes generics into concrete machine code. A crate that exports `fn foo<T>(x: T)` doesn't generate code until a downstream crate calls `foo::<String>()`. That means changing a leaf crate can trigger codegen in crates that use its generics. TS never has this problem.

**The workspace layout**:

```
Cargo.toml          # [workspace] members = ["crates/*"]
Cargo.lock          # single lockfile, like pnpm-lock.yaml
target/             # single output dir, shared by all crates
crates/
  rules/            # lib crate -- compiles to .rlib
  sprf/             # lib crate -- depends on rules
  cli/              # bin crate -- depends on everything, produces executable
```

**`cargo build -p sprefa`** builds only the CLI and its transitive deps. Like `tsc -b crates/cli`. Unchanged deps are skipped via fingerprinting.

**`cargo build --workspace`** builds everything. Like `tsc -b` at the root with all references.

**What triggers recompilation**: changing any `.rs` file in a crate recompiles that crate + everything that depends on it. Changing `Cargo.toml` (deps, features, profile) recompiles more broadly. Changing the profile settings (like we did with `opt-level`) recompiles all deps because the output is different.

That last point is why your build took so long -- the profile change invalidated every cached artifact.

---

## ASSISTANT

**`cargo build`** compiles into `target/debug/` (or `target/release/` with `--release`). Output stays local to the project. Like `tsc` outputting to `dist/`. You run the binary from `./target/debug/sprefa`.

**`cargo install --path crates/cli`** compiles with the **release** profile and copies the binary to `~/.cargo/bin/`. Like `npm install -g` but it compiles from source instead of downloading. It's meant for "put this on my PATH permanently."

The critical difference: `cargo install` defaults to `--release` (optimized). `cargo build` defaults to debug (fast compile, slow runtime). Release builds take longer because LLVM does aggressive optimization passes.

```
cargo build -p sprefa           -> target/debug/sprefa      (fast compile, debug)
cargo build -p sprefa --release -> target/release/sprefa    (slow compile, optimized)
cargo install --path crates/cli -> ~/.cargo/bin/sprefa      (same as --release, copies to PATH)
```

So when you ran `cargo install` after a debug build, it recompiled everything from scratch with different optimization flags. Two full builds, different profiles, different output dirs. That's why it took forever twice.

**What to do instead**: during development, just use `cargo build` and run from `target/debug/`. Only `cargo install` when you want a permanent system-wide binary. Or:

```bash
cargo build --release -p sprefa -p sprefa_sprf_lsp
ln -sf $(pwd)/target/release/sprefa ~/.cargo/bin/sprefa
ln -sf $(pwd)/target/release/sprf-lsp ~/.cargo/bin/sprf-lsp
```

Build release once, symlink to PATH. Incremental rebuilds with `cargo build --release` only recompile changed crates.

---

## ASSISTANT

Let me save that learning for next time.

---

## ASSISTANT

`--release` tells LLVM to optimize the output. Without it, LLVM does almost nothing -- just emits machine code fast.

| | debug (default) | release (`--release`) |
|---|---|---|
| compile speed | fast | 2-5x slower |
| runtime speed | slow (no inlining, no vectorization) | fast |
| binary size | large (debug symbols) | smaller (stripped) |
| output dir | `target/debug/` | `target/release/` |
| `opt-level` | 0 | 3 |

For a daemon scanning thousands of files, release mode matters -- the rayon parallelism and tree walking are measurably faster. For the LSP parsing one file, it doesn't.

During dev: `cargo build` (debug). When you want to actually run the daemon on real repos: `cargo build --release`.

---

## ASSISTANT

`Box<dyn Trait>` avoids monomorphization -- one copy of the function, vtable dispatch at runtime. So yes, fewer LLVM IR copies, faster compile. Slower runtime due to indirect calls and lost inlining.

`unsafe` doesn't help compile time at all. The borrow checker runs regardless -- `unsafe` just lets you ignore its errors in a block. The analysis cost is the same.

For this project, the compile bottleneck isn't your code's generics. It's the 300 dependency crates each doing their own monomorphization internally. `tokio`, `axum`, `sqlx` are template-heavy regardless of how you call them. Boxing your own trait objects saves seconds at most.

The actual compile time breakdown is roughly:

- 70% dependency crates (nothing you can do except use fewer)
- 20% LLVM optimization (only matters in release)
- 10% your code

Boxing everything in your 14 crates would shave maybe 10% off that 10%. Not worth the runtime cost or the code ugliness.

---

## ASSISTANT

The heavy hitters in the dep tree:

| Dep | What it pulls in | What you actually use |
|---|---|---|
| **tokio** | runtime, io, net, sync, time, macros | spawn, channels, file I/O |
| **axum** | hyper, tower, http, mime, matchit | 4 HTTP endpoints |
| **sqlx** | compile-time query macros, tokio-stream, futures | SQLite queries |
| **oxc_parser** | oxc_ast, oxc_allocator, oxc_span, simd-json | import/export extraction |
| **oxc_resolver** | tsconfig, node_modules, json-strip-comments | module resolution |
| **tower-lsp** | tower, hyper, http, dashmap, futures | LSP protocol |
| **syn** | proc-macro2, quote | Rust use-tree parsing |
| **git2** | libgit2-sys (C build) | ls-files, tags |
| **reqwest** | hyper-util, http-body, encoding_rs | CLI -> daemon HTTP calls |
| **notify** | kqueue/inotify, crossbeam | fs watching |

Realistic cuts:

1. **axum -> tiny-http or roll your own**. 4 routes don't need a framework. `std::net::TcpListener` + manual JSON parsing. Drops axum, tower, hyper, http, mime.

2. **reqwest -> ureq**. Sync HTTP client, no tokio dependency. Or just `std::net::TcpStream` for localhost calls.

3. **sqlx -> rusqlite**. No async, no compile-time checking, no futures stack. Direct C bindings to sqlite3. Faster compile, simpler code. You're not using async SQLite features anyway -- it's all `spawn_blocking`.

4. **tower-lsp -> lsp-server**. The rust-analyzer team's own LSP crate. Sync, no tower, no hyper. ~5 deps total vs tower-lsp's ~80.

5. **syn -> tree-sitter-rust**. C library, no proc-macro overhead. Or a focused hand-rolled `use` parser -- you only extract use trees and declarations, not the full AST.

6. **oxc_parser -> tree-sitter-javascript + tree-sitter-typescript**. C libraries. Or shell out to ast-grep for everything.

7. **git2 -> shell out to `git`**. `git ls-files`, `git tag --list`. Drops libgit2 C build entirely.

8. **tokio -> smol or std threads**. If you drop axum and sqlx's async, tokio has no remaining justification. The scan is rayon (sync). The watcher is notify callbacks. The server is 4 endpoints.

The nuclear option: drop tokio, axum, sqlx, reqwest, tower-lsp, syn. Replace with rusqlite, lsp-server, tree-sitter, ureq, std threads. Dep count goes from ~300 to maybe ~60. Build time drops proportionally.

The moderate option: swap sqlx -> rusqlite and tower-lsp -> lsp-server. Those two changes alone cut the futures/tower/hyper stack significantly.

---

## ASSISTANT

Faster to compile, same or less code to write. rusqlite is actually simpler than sqlx:

```rust
// sqlx (current)
let rows: Vec<(i64, String)> = sqlx::query_as("SELECT id, name FROM repos")
    .fetch_all(&pool).await?;

// rusqlite
let rows: Vec<(i64, String)> = conn.prepare("SELECT id, name FROM repos")?
    .query_map([], |row| Ok((row.get(0)?, row.get(1)?)))?
    .collect::<Result<_, _>>()?;
```

Similar line count, no async, no `.await`, no pool management. `lsp-server` is even more dramatic:

```rust
// tower-lsp (current) -- needs async trait, tokio runtime, tower service layer
#[tower_lsp::async_trait]
impl LanguageServer for SprfLsp {
    async fn completion(&self, params: CompletionParams) -> Result<...> { }
}

// lsp-server -- plain loop, no async, no traits
let (connection, io_threads) = Connection::stdio();
for msg in &connection.receiver {
    match msg {
        Message::Request(req) if req.method == "textDocument/completion" => {
            let params: CompletionParams = serde_json::from_value(req.params)?;
            // handle it, send response
        }
        _ => {}
    }
}
```

Less abstraction, less code, no async runtime. The pattern repeats across every swap. The "enterprise" Rust ecosystem wraps simple things in generic tower layers and async traits. The lightweight alternatives just do the thing directly.

---

## ASSISTANT

If you can read Rust, Zig reads like Rust minus the ceremony:

```zig
// no lifetime annotations, no trait bounds, no turbofish
fn walk(value: std.json.Value, steps: []const SelectStep) []MatchResult {
    // comptime generics instead of monomorphization
    // explicit allocators instead of hidden heap allocation
    // errdefer instead of Drop trait
}
```

| Rust | Zig |
|---|---|
| `Result<T, E>` | `!T` (error union, built into language) |
| `Option<T>` | `?T` |
| `.unwrap()` | `orelse unreachable` |
| `impl Trait` | `anytype` (comptime duck typing) |
| lifetimes `'a` | doesn't exist, manual memory |
| `Box<dyn Trait>` | `*Interface` (fat pointer, explicit) |
| `#[derive(Debug)]` | `std.fmt.format` just works |
| `cargo` | `zig build` (built in, ~instant) |
| 300 crate deps | C headers via `@cImport`, no package ecosystem needed |

The mental model shift: Rust prevents memory bugs at compile time through the type system. Zig prevents them through convention and tooling -- safety-checked allocators, `ReleaseSafe` mode that keeps bounds checks in release builds, address sanitizer built into the compiler. Less guarantee, more pragmatism.

The biggest adjustment: no RAII. No `Drop` trait, no automatic cleanup. You write `defer allocator.free(buf)` explicitly. It's manual but visible -- nothing happens behind your back.

For this project: sqlite3, tree-sitter, libgit2 are all C libraries. Zig consumes them with zero bindings. `@cImport(@cInclude("sqlite3.h"))` and you're calling `sqlite3_prepare_v2` directly. No build.rs, no -sys crate, no bindgen.

Build time for the whole project in Zig would be seconds. Not minutes. Seconds.

---

## ASSISTANT

Zig uses `comptime` where Rust uses generics. The code looks similar but the mechanism is different:

```zig
// Rust: monomorphized -- compiler stamps out a copy per T
fn max<T: Ord>(a: T, b: T) -> T {
    if a > b { a } else { b }
}

// Zig: comptime -- evaluated at compile time, one output
fn max(comptime T: type, a: T, b: T) T {
    return if (a > b) a else b;
}
```

Rust generates N copies of machine code (one per concrete T). Zig evaluates the function at compile time and produces one specialized version. Same result, but Zig's compiler does it in one pass without LLVM seeing N copies.

**Trait bounds -> comptime duck typing:**

```zig
// Rust
fn print_all<I: Iterator<Item = impl Display>>(iter: I) { ... }

// Zig -- if it has the method, it works. Error at call site if not.
fn printAll(iter: anytype) void {
    while (iter.next()) |item| {
        std.debug.print("{}", .{item});
    }
}
```

No trait declarations, no impl blocks, no orphan rules. If the type has `.next()`, it compiles. If not, you get a compile error pointing at the call site. Like Go interfaces but checked at compile time.

**Enums:**

```zig
// Rust
enum SelectStep {
    File { pattern: String },
    Object { entries: Vec<ObjectEntry> },
    Any,
}

// Zig -- tagged unions, same concept
const SelectStep = union(enum) {
    file: struct { pattern: []const u8 },
    object: struct { entries: []ObjectEntry },
    any: void,
};

// exhaustive switch, same as Rust match
switch (step) {
    .file => |f| ...,
    .object => |o| ...,
    .any => ...,
}
```

**Error handling:**

```zig
// Rust
fn parse(input: &str) -> Result<Program, anyhow::Error> { ... }
let program = parse(input)?;

// Zig -- same ? operator, errors are a built-in union type
fn parse(input: []const u8) !Program { ... }
const program = try parse(input);
// or
const program = parse(input) catch |err| { ... };
```

**The big difference: no ownership system.** Rust's `&`, `&mut`, lifetimes, `Clone`, `Arc` -- none of that. You pass pointers around and manage memory yourself:

```zig
// allocate
var list = try allocator.alloc(u8, 1024);
defer allocator.free(list);  // manual RAII

// pass a slice (pointer + length, like Rust &[T])
fn process(data: []const u8) void { ... }
```

The tradeoff: Zig trusts you to free memory. Rust proves you will. For a tool like sprefa where the lifetime patterns are straightforward (scan a file, extract refs, write to DB, free) the borrow checker is overhead you don't need.

---

## ASSISTANT

Same thing, `switch` is exhaustive:

```zig
switch (step) {
    .file => |f| handleFile(f.pattern),
    .object => |o| {
        for (o.entries) |entry| {
            // nested switch on entry.key
            switch (entry.key) {
                .exact => |s| ...,
                .glob => |g| ...,
                .wildcard => ...,
                .capture => |c| ...,
            }
        }
    },
    .any => recurse(value, remaining_steps),
    .array => |a| {
        for (value.array.items) |item| {
            try walkInner(item, a.item);
        }
    },
    .leaf => |l| {
        if (l.capture) |name| captures.put(name, value);
    },
}
```

Add a variant to the union, compiler breaks every switch that doesn't handle it. Same guarantee as Rust. The syntax is slightly different -- `|payload|` captures the inner value instead of destructuring in the arm pattern -- but the semantics are identical.

The `matches!` macro pattern from Rust doesn't exist, but inline checks work:

```zig
// Rust
if matches!(&key, KeyMatcher::Exact(s) if s == "**") { ... }

// Zig
if (key == .exact and std.mem.eql(u8, key.exact, "**")) { ... }
```

---

## ASSISTANT

The dot is enum inference. Zig infers the enum type from context so you don't repeat yourself:

```zig
const Color = enum { red, green, blue };

// full form
var c: Color = Color.red;

// inferred -- compiler knows c is Color, so .red is unambiguous
var c: Color = .red;

// in switch -- compiler knows you're switching on a Color
switch (c) {
    .red => ...,    // Color.red
    .green => ...,  // Color.green
    .blue => ...,   // Color.blue
}

// in function args
fn paint(color: Color) void { ... }
paint(.red);  // no need to write Color.red
```

It's like Rust's `Self::Variant` shorthand but everywhere. Anywhere the compiler knows the type, you just write `.variant`. In switches, comparisons, assignments, function calls.

The `==` comparison with `.exact` is the same thing -- checking which variant a tagged union holds without spelling out `SelectStep.exact`.

---

## ASSISTANT

Yeah. Zig is strongly typed, it just infers aggressively and uses different syntax:

```zig
// primitives
const x: u32 = 42;
const y: f64 = 3.14;
const name: []const u8 = "hello";  // string = byte slice

// structs (like Rust, no class)
const Rule = struct {
    name: []const u8,
    select: []SelectStep,
    create_matches: []MatchDef,
    confidence: ?f64,           // ? = optional, like Option<f64>
};

// tagged union (like Rust enum with data)
const SelectStep = union(enum) {
    file: FileStep,
    object: ObjectStep,
    any: void,
    leaf: LeafStep,
};

// plain enum (no data)
const Tag = enum { json, re, ast, repo, branch, fs };

// type aliases
const Program = []Statement;

// function types
const WalkFn = *const fn ([]const u8) ![]MatchResult;
```

The unfamiliar parts:

- `[]const u8` = immutable byte slice = Rust's `&[u8]` or `&str`
- `[]u8` = mutable byte slice = Rust's `&mut [u8]`
- `?T` = optional = Rust's `Option<T>`
- `!T` = error union = Rust's `Result<T, E>`
- `*T` = single pointer = Rust's `&T`
- `[*]T` = many pointer (C-style) = Rust's `*const T`

No generics syntax like `<T>`. Instead `comptime T: type` as a parameter. No lifetimes because no borrow checker. No trait bounds because comptime duck typing. The types exist, the ceremony around them doesn't.

---

## ASSISTANT

Runtime? No. Zig compiles to the same LLVM backend as Rust (or its own backend for faster builds). Same optimizations, same machine code quality. The Zig compiler itself is written in Zig and self-hosts in 3 seconds -- it's not leaving performance on the table.

Benchmarks put Zig within single-digit percentages of Rust and C for equivalent code. Sometimes faster because you control the allocator per call site instead of using a global allocator.

What Rust can theoretically do better:

- **Aliasing optimizations** via `&mut` guarantees -- LLVM can assume no aliasing through `noalias` annotations. In practice this rarely matters outside tight numerical loops.
- **Auto-vectorization** -- same LLVM, same result.
- **Move semantics** -- Rust moves by default, no copies. Zig copies structs by value unless you use pointers explicitly. For small structs it's the same. For large structs you just pass a pointer.

What Zig can do better:

- **Allocator control** -- arena allocator for a scan pass, free everything at once. No per-object Drop calls, no reference counting.
- **No hidden costs** -- no Arc, no vtable, no panic unwinding machinery, no thread-local storage for the runtime. You see every allocation.
- **SIMD built into the language** -- vector types are first class, not a library.

For sprefa specifically -- scanning files, walking JSON trees, writing to SQLite -- the runtime performance would be indistinguishable. The workload is I/O bound (reading files, writing DB), not compute bound. The difference in iteration speed (seconds vs minutes to compile) would matter far more than any runtime delta.

---

## ASSISTANT

Zig defaults smaller. A hello world:

| | Zig | Rust |
|---|---|---|
| debug | ~5 KB | ~4 MB |
| release | ~5 KB | ~300 KB |
| release stripped | ~2 KB | ~200 KB |

Rust's binary includes panic unwinding machinery, the formatting infrastructure (`std::fmt`), and the global allocator. Zig includes none of that unless you use it.

For a real project like sprefa, the gap narrows because you're using the same C libraries (sqlite3, tree-sitter, libgit2) which have fixed size regardless. But the Rust overhead is still there -- every `?` operator pulls in the panic/unwind machinery, every `println!` pulls in the formatting stack, `tokio` alone adds ~1MB to the binary.

Rough estimate for sprefa:

| | Zig | Rust |
|---|---|---|
| release stripped | ~3-5 MB | ~15-20 MB |

The Zig binary is mostly the C libraries. The Rust binary is the C libraries plus the async runtime, the tower stack, the serde machinery, the proc-macro output, and the monomorphized generic soup.

---

## ASSISTANT

Right. Benchmark the current Rust implementation first, then you have a baseline to compare against if you ever port.

The metrics that matter for sprefa:

- **Scan throughput**: files/sec on this workspace (and a large repo like chromium or linux)
- **Walk engine**: matches/sec for json destructuring against a big `values.yaml` or `package-lock.json`
- **SQLite flush**: refs/sec bulk insert
- **Import resolution**: resolves/sec through oxc_resolver
- **Link resolution**: links/sec for match_links query
- **Memory**: peak RSS during scan

`criterion` is the standard Rust benchmarking crate. Produces statistically significant measurements with confidence intervals. The benchmarks become the regression suite -- if a port to Zig is slower on any axis, the numbers show it.

That's a separate session though.

---

## ASSISTANT

The borrow checker is genuinely cool for one specific scenario: shared mutable state across threads. That's it. That's the thing no other language does at compile time.

```rust
// This won't compile -- borrow checker catches data race at compile time
let mut data = vec![1, 2, 3];
std::thread::spawn(|| data.push(4));  // capture &mut
println!("{:?}", data);                // capture &
// error: cannot borrow as immutable because also borrowed as mutable
```

Go catches this at runtime with `-race`. C doesn't catch it at all. Zig doesn't catch it at all. Zig and C trust you to use a mutex. The borrow checker proves you did.

For sprefa's scan engine -- rayon splitting file lists across cores, each thread extracting refs and writing to a shared output buffer -- the borrow checker is doing real work. A bug there would be a silent data race that corrupts the index intermittently. Hard to reproduce, hard to debug.

For everything else in the project -- the parser, the LSP, the CLI, the rule engine, the watcher -- it's single-threaded or trivially concurrent (one thread per watched repo). The borrow checker adds ceremony with no safety benefit.

The honest split:

| Worth it | Not worth it |
|---|---|
| rayon parallel scan | CLI argument parsing |
| concurrent DB writes | .sprf parser |
| shared scanner state | LSP (one file at a time) |
| watcher -> scanner channel | rule engine (single-threaded walk) |

So maybe 20% of the codebase benefits from the borrow checker. The other 80% is paying the compile tax for a guarantee it doesn't need. The sexy native binaries come from LLVM, not from the borrow checker -- Zig and C produce equally fast binaries.

---

## ASSISTANT

Zig's `comptime` is its metaprogramming story. It's powerful but different from Rust's proc macros:

```zig
// comptime runs arbitrary Zig code at compile time
fn makeSelectSteps(comptime n: usize) [n]SelectStep {
    var steps: [n]SelectStep = undefined;
    // actual loop running at compile time
    for (0..n) |i| {
        steps[i] = .any;
    }
    return steps;
}

// type reflection -- inspect types at compile time
fn fieldNames(comptime T: type) []const []const u8 {
    const info = @typeInfo(T);
    return switch (info) {
        .@"struct" => |s| s.fields.map(|f| f.name),
        else => &.{},
    };
}

// generate serialization, validation, whatever at compile time
fn autoSerialize(comptime T: type, value: T) []const u8 {
    const fields = @typeInfo(T).@"struct".fields;
    inline for (fields) |field| {
        // this loop unrolls at compile time
        // each iteration knows the field name and type
    }
}
```

For autodrop, you build it with `defer`:

```zig
// Rust: Drop trait, implicit
// Zig: explicit, but you can wrap it

fn Arena() type {
    return struct {
        allocations: std.ArrayList(*anyopaque),

        pub fn create(self: *@This(), comptime T: type) !*T {
            const ptr = try self.backing.create(T);
            try self.allocations.append(ptr);
            return ptr;
        }

        pub fn deinit(self: *@This()) void {
            // free everything at once
            for (self.allocations.items) |ptr| self.backing.destroy(ptr);
        }
    };
}

// usage
var arena = Arena().init(allocator);
defer arena.deinit();  // autodrop -- everything freed when scope exits

const rule = try arena.create(Rule);
const step = try arena.create(SelectStep);
// no individual frees needed
```

For custom checks:

```zig
// comptime assertions -- like static_assert
fn validateRule(comptime rule: Rule) void {
    if (rule.select.len == 0) {
        @compileError("rule must have at least one select step");
    }
    if (rule.create_matches.len == 0) {
        @compileError("rule must have at least one match slot");
    }
}
```

The gap vs Rust: no proc macros (no `#[derive]`), no trait-based ad-hoc polymorphism (no `impl Display for MyType`). You write the serialization/formatting code explicitly or generate it with `comptime`. More code, more visible, compiles instantly.

The arena pattern above is how Zig projects handle the "I want RAII" problem. Allocate into an arena, defer its destruction, done. No reference counting, no Drop chains, no hidden control flow. The scan engine would allocate all refs for one file into an arena, flush to DB, free the arena. One `deinit` call instead of thousands of individual drops.

---

## ASSISTANT

Yeah. Struct fields use `:` and end with `,`. Functions inside structs are just declarations, no separator needed:

```zig
const Foo = struct {
    // fields -- comma terminated
    name: []const u8,
    count: u32,
    parent: ?*Foo,

    // methods -- no comma, no separator
    pub fn init(name: []const u8) Foo {
        return .{ .name = name, .count = 0, .parent = null };
    }

    pub fn increment(self: *Foo) void {
        self.count += 1;
    }
};
```

Fields are data declarations (need the comma like struct fields in C). Functions are definitions (no comma, like functions anywhere else). The struct body is both a data layout and a namespace. Methods take `self` explicitly as the first parameter -- no implicit `this` or `&self` magic.

```zig
var f = Foo.init("hello");  // "static" call
f.increment();              // method call -- compiler passes &f as self
```

---

## ASSISTANT

Zig's learning path is short. The [language reference](https://ziglang.org/documentation/master/) is one page and covers everything. No book needed. Andrew Kelley (the creator) has a few talks that cover the philosophy in under an hour.

But yeah, finish what's in front of you first. The Rust is written, the tests pass, the LSP and highlighting just landed. Porting to Zig is a future session if the compile times stay intolerable after the dep tree gets trimmed.

For now: the build should be done or close to done. Once it finishes, incremental rebuilds will be seconds and the pain stops.

---

## ASSISTANT

And that transfers 1:1. The `SelectStep` enum, `Rule` struct, `ObjectEntry`, `KeyMatcher`, `LinkPredicate` -- all of that is the same shapes in Zig, Go, or anything else. The data model is the hard part and it's language-agnostic.

The parser is hand-rolled recursive descent -- same algorithm in any language. The walk engine is a recursive function over a tagged union. The SQL schema doesn't change. The `.sprf` grammar doesn't change.

A port would be mechanical transcription of types and logic, not a redesign. The months of work were figuring out what the types should be.

---

## ASSISTANT

D is the "what if C++ was designed by someone sane" language. Fast compile times, GC by default but optional, templates that actually work:

```d
// looks like Rust and C++ had a readable child
struct SelectStep {
    enum Tag { file, object, any, leaf, array }
    Tag tag;
    // ...
}

// templates compile fast -- no LLVM monomorphization explosion
auto walk(V)(V value, SelectStep[] steps) { ... }

// mixins -- string-level metaprogramming, comptime code generation
mixin("int x = 3;");  // cursed but powerful

// pattern matching via switch
final switch (step.tag) {
    case Tag.file: ...
    case Tag.object: ...
    // exhaustive -- compiler errors on missing cases
}
```

| | D | Zig | Rust |
|---|---|---|---|
| compile speed | fast (~Go tier) | instant | slow |
| GC | default on, optional off | none | none |
| C interop | `extern(C)` + header import | `@cImport` | bindgen + -sys crate |
| metaprogramming | mixins + CTFE + templates | comptime | proc macros |
| package manager | dub (small ecosystem) | build.zig (tiny ecosystem) | cargo (huge ecosystem) |
| binary size | medium | small | large |
| community | small, shrinking | small, growing | large |

The honest problem with D: the ecosystem is dying. It peaked around 2015. The standard library (Phobos) is comprehensive but the third-party ecosystem is thin. Finding a maintained SQLite binding, tree-sitter binding, or LSP library is harder than in Zig or Rust. You'd be writing more from scratch.

Zig's community is small but has momentum. D's community is small and losing it. For a new project in 2026, D is a harder bet.

---

## ASSISTANT

D is genuinely well-designed. The ranges system, UFCS (uniform function call syntax), compile-time function evaluation that just works without a special keyword. It solved problems in 2007 that Rust is still arguing about in RFCs.

```d
// UFCS -- any free function chains like a method
"hello world".split(" ").map!(toUpper).array;

// compile-time function evaluation -- just runs normal code
enum x = factorial(10);  // computed at compile time, no special syntax

// scope guards -- like defer but with conditions  
scope(exit) file.close();
scope(failure) rollback();
scope(success) commit();
```

The scope guards alone are better than Rust's Drop or Zig's defer. `scope(failure)` only runs on error. Try doing that cleanly in Rust.

It just lost the adoption war. Right language, wrong decade, no corporate sponsor. Go had Google. Rust had Mozilla. D had Walter Bright's personal conviction and that wasn't enough to build critical mass.

---

## ASSISTANT

Nowhere worth carrying it to. D lost because of ecosystem network effects, not technical merit. That gap only widens with time. Writing new projects in D in 2026 means writing every library yourself.

The torch worth carrying: the ideas. D's scope guards, UFCS, and CTFE influenced Zig directly. Zig's `defer`/`errdefer` is D's `scope(exit)`/`scope(failure)` simplified. The good parts migrated.

If you want to champion an underdog systems language with D's design sensibility but actual momentum, that's Zig. If you want fast compiles and don't need manual memory management, that's Go. If you want to suffer beautifully, stay in Rust.

The real torch to carry is the one you're already carrying -- sprefa's data model and the `.sprf` DSL. That's language-portable. Ship it in Rust now, port it to whatever compiles in under 5 seconds when you're ready.

---

## ASSISTANT

sccache.

First build: every crate compiles from source, sccache stores the result. 300 crates, cold cache, minutes.

Second build: sccache recognizes the same inputs (source hash + compiler flags + features) and returns the cached artifact. Skips compilation entirely. The "build" is just cache lookups and linking.

```
first:   source -> rustc -> LLVM -> .rlib  (slow, cached by sccache)
second:  source -> sccache hit -> .rlib    (instant)
```

Incremental builds (change one file) are fast for a different reason: cargo's fingerprinting skips unchanged crates entirely, and only the changed crate + its dependents recompile. sccache may still help for the dependents if it's seen that exact input before.

The two caches stack: cargo skips what hasn't changed, sccache skips what it's compiled before. After one full build, both caches are warm and everything is fast until you change compiler flags, clean target, or add a new dependency.

---

## ASSISTANT

```bash
sprefa reset
```

That's the command we just added. Drops the DB file + WAL/SHM, recreates with fresh schema.

## ASSISTANT

Vite's sub-second HMR is a legitimate benchmark for what dev tooling should feel like. The frustration is warranted.

## ASSISTANT

Rolldown proving that Rust can power instant dev tooling makes the compile time story worse, not better. The tool you use to build JS in milliseconds takes minutes to build itself. The irony is structural.

The language history interest is a good instinct for someone building a cross-language indexing engine. Understanding why Go chose fat interfaces, why Zig chose explicit allocators, why D chose scope guards -- that's directly applicable to designing a DSL that has to describe patterns across all of them.

---

## ASSISTANT

Zig could match Rolldown's runtime speed. Same LLVM backend, same optimization passes, same machine code output. For a bundler hot path (parsing, transforming, emitting JS), the bottleneck is the algorithm and data layout, not the language.

Where Rust has an edge in that specific domain: oxc. The oxc parser is hand-tuned Rust with arena allocation and zero-copy string handling. It's faster than tree-sitter for JS/TS because it's purpose-built. Porting oxc to Zig would produce equivalent speed but that's a massive undertaking -- oxc is ~100k lines.

The actual speed advantage Rolldown has isn't Rust vs other languages. It's:

1. oxc parser (fastest JS parser that exists, in any language)
2. Parallelism via rayon (borrow checker makes this safe to ship)
3. Arena allocators reducing allocation pressure
4. Shared AST nodes across transform passes (zero-copy)

Points 1, 3, 4 are achievable in Zig or C. Point 2 is where the borrow checker earns its compile tax -- shipping parallel code to millions of users without data race bugs. For an open source tool used by thousands of projects, that guarantee has real value.

For sprefa, used by you on your machine, the calculus is different.

---

## ASSISTANT

Not built in, but Zig's standard library has a thread pool:

```zig
// Zig's std thread pool -- similar to rayon but explicit
var pool = std.Thread.Pool.init(.{ .n_jobs = 8 });
defer pool.deinit();

// parallel map over files
var results = try allocator.alloc(ExtractResult, files.len);
for (files, 0..) |file, i| {
    pool.spawn(extractFile, .{ file, &results[i] });
}
pool.waitAll();
```

Rayon's magic is the work-stealing scheduler and the parallel iterator API (`files.par_iter().map(...)`). Zig doesn't have that abstraction. You manage the thread pool and task distribution yourself.

The difference in practice:

```rust
// Rayon -- one line, automatic work stealing
let refs: Vec<_> = files.par_iter()
    .flat_map(|f| extract(f))
    .collect();
```

```zig
// Zig -- explicit but not much more code
var wg = std.Thread.WaitGroup{};
for (files) |file| {
    wg.start();
    try pool.spawn(struct {
        fn run(f: File, out: *Results, w: *WaitGroup) void {
            defer w.finish();
            const refs = extract(f);
            out.append(refs);
        }
    }.run, .{ file, &results, &wg });
}
wg.wait();
```

More lines, same throughput. The work-stealing part is the only real loss -- rayon automatically rebalances when some files take longer than others. Zig's pool is fixed distribution. For file scanning where files are roughly equal size, the difference is negligible. For heterogeneous workloads (one huge file, many tiny ones), rayon adapts better.

There's also [zap](https://github.com/yrashk/zap) -- a third-party Zig work-stealing thread pool inspired by rayon. Small library, not a 200-crate dependency tree.

---

## ASSISTANT

The std library is substantial actually:

```
std.fs           -- file I/O, directory walking
std.net          -- TCP/UDP sockets, listening, connecting
std.http         -- HTTP client AND server (built in!)
std.json         -- JSON parse/stringify
std.mem          -- allocation, slicing, searching
std.fmt          -- formatting/printing
std.Thread       -- threads, pool, mutex, rwlock, atomics
std.process      -- spawn child processes
std.crypto       -- SHA, AES, HMAC, etc
std.compress     -- gzip, zlib, zstd
std.tar          -- tar archive read/write
std.Uri          -- URL parsing
std.testing      -- test runner (built into the language)
std.log          -- structured logging
std.heap         -- arena, page, general purpose allocators
std.sort         -- sorting
std.hash_map     -- hash maps
std.ArrayList    -- dynamic arrays
std.BoundedArray -- stack-allocated dynamic array
std.Random       -- RNG
std.time         -- timestamps, timers
std.os           -- kqueue, epoll, io_uring, direct syscalls
std.c            -- libc interop
```

HTTP server and client in std. JSON in std. Threads and pools in std. Crypto in std. The Go approach.

For sprefa specifically, what you'd need from outside std:

| Need | Source |
|---|---|
| SQLite | `@cImport("sqlite3.h")` -- C library, zero binding |
| tree-sitter | `@cImport("tree-sitter.h")` -- C library, zero binding |
| glob matching | ~50 lines to write, or pull a small lib |
| regex | std doesn't have it -- PCRE via `@cImport`, or a small Zig lib |
| file watching | kqueue/inotify via `std.os` directly |

The dep count for sprefa in Zig: sqlite3.c, tree-sitter.c, maybe pcre.c. Three C files. No package manager needed.

---

## ASSISTANT

No. `std.Uri` parses URLs into scheme/host/port/path/query/fragment. No route matching, no path params, no `:id` extraction.

But the HTTP server is in std, so you'd pattern match the path yourself:

```zig
var server = std.http.Server.init(allocator, .{});
while (server.accept()) |req| {
    const path = req.target;
    if (std.mem.startsWith(u8, path, "/repos/")) {
        const name = path[7..];  // everything after /repos/
        // done
    }
}
```

For 4 endpoints that's fine. For a real API framework, there's nothing in std. You'd write a small router or use one of the few Zig web libs (httpz, zap -- both tiny).

Compared to Go where `http.ServeMux` handles patterns with `{id}` wildcards since Go 1.22, it's a gap. But a smaller gap than Rust where the answer is "install axum and 80 transitive deps."

---

## ASSISTANT

Zig compiles C directly. No bindings layer, no code generation, no intermediate crate.

When Rust uses sqlite3:

```
sqlite3.c -> libsqlite3-sys (build.rs compiles C, generates bindings)
          -> rusqlite (safe Rust wrapper, error types, trait impls)
          -> your code
```

When Zig uses sqlite3:

```
sqlite3.c -> @cImport -> your code
```

`@cImport` reads the C header and makes the functions callable. No wrapper crate, no bindgen, no build.rs, no -sys crate. The Zig compiler literally contains a C compiler (it can compile C and C++ files as a build step).

Same for tree-sitter, libgit2, pcre, anything with a C API. Rust needs a -sys crate that vendors or links the C library, plus a safe wrapper crate on top. Two crates per C dependency minimum. Zig needs the `.h` file.

The Rust ecosystem is large because every C library gets wrapped twice (unsafe bindings + safe API), every abstraction gets a crate, every crate pulls in error handling + serde + async compat. Zig sidesteps all of it by treating C as a first-class citizen rather than a foreign country that requires a visa.

---

## ASSISTANT

```zig
const c = @cImport(@cInclude("sqlite3.h"));

pub fn main() !void {
    var db: ?*c.sqlite3 = null;
    _ = c.sqlite3_open("index.db", &db);
    defer _ = c.sqlite3_close(db);

    var stmt: ?*c.sqlite3_stmt = null;
    _ = c.sqlite3_prepare_v2(
        db,
        "SELECT id, value, norm FROM strings WHERE norm = ?",
        -1,
        &stmt,
        null,
    );
    defer _ = c.sqlite3_finalize(stmt);

    _ = c.sqlite3_bind_text(stmt, 1, "mypackage", -1, null);

    while (c.sqlite3_step(stmt) == c.SQLITE_ROW) {
        const id = c.sqlite3_column_int64(stmt, 0);
        const value = c.sqlite3_column_text(stmt, 1);
        std.debug.print("id={} value={s}\n", .{ id, value });
    }
}
```

That's it. No crate, no dependency, no wrapper. The C API directly. Build with:

```bash
zig build-exe main.zig -lsqlite3
```

Or vendor `sqlite3.c` and compile it as part of your build:

```bash
zig build-exe main.zig sqlite3.c -lc
```

One command. Zig compiles your code and the C file together. No build.rs, no pkg-config, no linking dance.

---

## ASSISTANT

Rust's leg on C:

1. Memory safety guarantees across thread boundaries
2. That's it

C has no bounds checking, no use-after-free protection, no data race prevention. Zig calling C directly inherits all of C's unsafety at the boundary. Every `sqlite3_column_text` returns a `[*c]const u8` -- a raw C pointer that could be null, dangling, or pointing to freed memory. Zig won't stop you from using it wrong.

Rust's -sys + wrapper crate model exists specifically to put a safe type boundary around C's chaos. `rusqlite` returns `Result<String>` instead of a nullable pointer. You can't forget to check for null because the type system forces you through `?` or `match`.

But here's the thing: sqlite3 is one of the most battle-tested C libraries on earth. It doesn't return dangling pointers. Its API contract is well-defined and has been stable for 20 years. Wrapping it in two layers of Rust types to protect against bugs that don't exist in the library is defensive programming against a threat model that doesn't apply.

The borrow checker earns its keep when YOU write concurrent mutable code. It doesn't add value when you're calling into a C library that already manages its own memory correctly. And for sprefa, most of the dependency weight is exactly that -- safe wrappers around C libraries that don't need wrapping.

---

## ASSISTANT

No. Nothing like rxjs in Zig. The async story is deliberately minimal:

Zig had `async`/`await` built in, then **removed it** in 0.11 (2023). Andrew Kelley decided the implementation wasn't good enough and pulled it to redo it properly. As of 2026 it's still not back. The current model is:

- `std.Thread.Pool` for parallelism
- `std.os` for `io_uring`/`kqueue`/`epoll` directly
- Callbacks and manual state machines

No streams, no operators, no composition. You write event loops by hand.

For rxjs-style composition, your options in systems languages:

| Language | Stream composition |
|---|---|
| **Rust** | `tokio-stream`, `futures::Stream`, decent but verbose |
| **Go** | channels + goroutines + `select`. Manual but clean. |
| **Zig** | nothing. write the loop yourself |
| **D** | `std.algorithm` + ranges. Closest to rxjs in a systems lang |
| **C** | lol |

D's ranges are actually the closest thing to rxjs pipelines in a compiled language:

```d
files
    .filter!(f => f.ext == ".ts")
    .map!(f => extract(f))
    .joiner
    .each!(ref => db.insert(ref));
```

For sprefa's use case -- file events from a watcher, debounce, classify, plan, apply -- you'd hand-roll that pipeline in Zig. It's maybe 50 lines of explicit state machine vs 10 lines of composed operators. More code, same result, compiles instantly.

---

## ASSISTANT

You could. Build rxjs-style operators at comptime:

```zig
fn pipe(input: anytype, comptime stages: anytype) PipeResult(stages) {
    var state = input;
    inline for (stages) |stage| {
        state = stage(state);
    }
    return state;
}

// usage
const result = pipe(file_events, .{
    debounce(100),
    filter(isRelevant),
    map(classify),
    flatMap(planRewrites),
});
```

`inline for` unrolls at compile time. Each stage is type-checked against the previous stage's output. Zero runtime overhead -- the compiler inlines everything into a single function body. No vtables, no trait objects, no heap allocation for the pipeline itself.

The type plumbing gets verbose though. Each operator returns a different type (the output of `filter` is different from the output of `map`), and you'd need to propagate that through comptime type functions. Doable, ugly, and nobody's built the library yet.

The Zig community's answer to "why not build X with comptime" is usually "because someone has to write it first and the community is small." The building blocks exist. The composed library doesn't. That's the ecosystem gap -- not a language limitation.

Someone could build a comptime stream library in Zig in a few hundred lines. In Rust it took the `futures` crate years and thousands of lines because async traits and pinning made it structurally complex. Zig's comptime approach would be simpler to implement but nobody's done it yet.

---

## ASSISTANT

No GC. Manual memory management, like C.

Every allocation goes through an explicit `allocator` parameter:

```zig
fn extract(allocator: std.mem.Allocator, file: File) ![]Ref {
    var refs = std.ArrayList(Ref).init(allocator);
    // ...
    return refs.toOwnedSlice();
}
```

You choose the allocator per call site:

```zig
// arena -- bulk free, no individual frees
var arena = std.heap.ArenaAllocator.init(std.heap.page_allocator);
defer arena.deinit();  // frees everything at once

// general purpose -- malloc/free style
const gpa = std.heap.GeneralPurposeAllocator(.{}){};

// fixed buffer -- zero heap, stack only
var buf: [4096]u8 = undefined;
var fba = std.heap.FixedBufferAllocator.init(&buf);
```

The arena pattern is the killer feature for sprefa's scan loop:

```zig
for (files) |file| {
    var arena = ArenaAllocator.init(page_allocator);
    defer arena.deinit();  // one free per file, not per ref

    const refs = try extract(arena.allocator(), file);
    try flushToDb(db, refs);
    // arena.deinit() frees all refs at once
}
```

No GC pauses, no reference counting, no Drop chains. Allocate fast, free in bulk. For a scan engine touching thousands of files, this is faster than Rust's per-object Drop semantics.

---

## ASSISTANT

Yeah. It's a region of memory you bump-allocate into (fast, just increment a pointer) and free all at once (fast, one operation). No per-object bookkeeping.

```
malloc/free:    alloc A, alloc B, alloc C, free C, free A, free B
                (6 operations, fragmentation, overhead per object)

arena:          alloc A, alloc B, alloc C, free everything
                (4 operations, zero fragmentation, zero per-object cost)
```

The tradeoff: you can't free individual objects. It's all or nothing. Works perfectly when the lifetime is scoped -- process one file, free everything, next file. Doesn't work when objects have different lifetimes that interleave.

Rust has arenas too (`bumpalo`, `typed-arena`) but they fight the borrow checker because the arena owns memory that references point into. Zig doesn't have that problem because there's no borrow checker to fight.

---

## ASSISTANT

Per-object malloc is 2N operations (N allocs + N frees) plus bookkeeping overhead per allocation (size headers, free lists, fragmentation).

Arena is N+1: N allocs (just pointer bumps, near free) + 1 bulk free.

The pointer bump is the key. Regular malloc searches free lists, splits blocks, updates metadata. Arena bump allocation is:

```
ptr = current;
current += size;
return ptr;
```

Three instructions. Compared to `malloc`'s hundreds of instructions per call for free list traversal and coalescing. For a scan pass allocating thousands of small `Ref` structs, the difference is measurable.

---

## ASSISTANT

The arena grabs pages from the OS in chunks (usually 4KB or bigger) and bump-allocates within them. When a page fills up, it grabs another. You don't set a limit upfront:

```zig
// no size specified -- grows as needed
var arena = std.heap.ArenaAllocator.init(std.heap.page_allocator);
defer arena.deinit();

// allocate 10 bytes -- bumps pointer within current page
const a = try arena.allocator().alloc(u8, 10);
// allocate 8000 bytes -- doesn't fit, arena grabs a new page, bumps there
const b = try arena.allocator().alloc(u8, 8000);

// deinit returns ALL pages to the OS at once
```

```
page 1 [AAAAAAAAAA..............] <- first alloc, rest is free space
page 2 [BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB........] <- second alloc grabbed new page

deinit: free page 1, free page 2. done.
```

The arena maintains a linked list of pages. You never think about limits. It grows until you `deinit`, then everything disappears. The backing allocator (`page_allocator` here) is what actually talks to the OS.

---

## ASSISTANT

Rust has `#[global_allocator]` to swap the system allocator:

```rust
#[global_allocator]
static ALLOC: jemallocator::Jemalloc = jemallocator::Jemalloc;
```

That's global though -- everything in the process uses it. You can't say "this function uses arena, that function uses malloc" at the call site like Zig.

Rust also has the `Allocator` trait (nightly, unstable since 2016, still not stabilized in 2026):

```rust
// nightly only
let arena = Arena::new();
let mut vec = Vec::new_in(&arena);  // this vec allocates from the arena
```

The API exists but it's behind a feature gate that has been unstable for a decade. Same story as async in std -- the Rust team won't commit to stabilizing it.

So in practice: Rust gives you one global allocator for the whole binary. Zig gives you per-call-site allocator choice as a standard pattern. The `bumpalo` crate exists for arena allocation in Rust but it's a third-party workaround for a missing language feature.

This is another instance of the same governance problem. Per-allocator collections would reduce memory overhead and improve cache locality for exactly the kind of batch processing sprefa does. The design is done, the implementation exists on nightly, nobody will pull the trigger on stabilization.

---

## ASSISTANT

oxc uses `bumpalo` (arena allocator crate) for exactly this pattern:

```rust
let arena = bumpalo::Bump::new();
let ast = parser.parse_in(&arena, source_text);
// entire AST lives in one contiguous arena
// drop the arena, everything freed at once
```

The AST nodes, strings, vectors -- all bump-allocated into one region. No per-node `Box<T>`, no reference counting, no individual frees. Same pattern as Zig's arena but fighting the borrow checker to do it.

The rest of oxc's speed:

1. **Hand-written recursive descent parser** -- no parser generator overhead, branch prediction friendly
2. **Arena-allocated AST** -- cache-local, one bulk free
3. **Zero-copy strings** -- `&'arena str` pointing into the arena, not `String` clones
4. **No visitor pattern** -- direct match on enum variants, no vtable dispatch
5. **Scope analysis in the same pass** -- single-pass where most parsers do two

It's fast despite Rust, not because of Rust. The allocator API they need is unstable so they use a third-party crate. The borrow checker fights arena references so they use `unsafe` internally. The techniques are the same things you'd do in C or Zig, just with more ceremony to satisfy the type system.

The borrow checker's contribution: oxc ships to millions of users via Rolldown/Vite. The safe public API prevents consumers from holding dangling references into a freed arena. That matters at library scale. For internal implementation, they `unsafe` their way around it like everyone else.

---

## ASSISTANT

Yeah. tsc is 1.5 million lines of TypeScript checking TypeScript. A self-hosted compiler with no compilation, interpreting itself through V8 every time it runs. It's architecturally incapable of being fast.

A Zig rewrite of tsc would:

- Parse TypeScript via hand-written recursive descent (fast, like oxc)
- Arena allocate the AST per file (bulk free after checking)
- Call into tree-sitter-typescript via `@cImport` if you want incremental
- Type check with direct pointer-chasing over arena-allocated type nodes
- Compile to a single static binary, ~5MB, instant startup
- Parallel file checking via `std.Thread.Pool`
- Build itself in seconds

The Go team proved the model works -- `gopls` rewrote Go's type checker in Go and it's fast. But Go's type system is simple. TypeScript's type system is Turing-complete. That's the hard part -- not the language choice, but the type checker logic for conditional types, mapped types, template literal types, and the inference algorithm.

stc (Zig-based TS type checker) was attempted by the guy behind swc. He abandoned it because TypeScript's type system semantics are underspecified -- the spec is the implementation. You'd need to reverse-engineer tsc's behavior from test cases rather than from a formal spec. The language choice was fine. The specification gap killed it.

So: Zig is the right tool. The problem is that TypeScript's type system is defined by "whatever tsc does," and replicating that is a multi-year effort regardless of what language you write it in.

## ASSISTANT

The `regex-key` pattern is too greedy. It matches `re:` until it hits `:` or `}` or `]`, but `]` in the regex character class `[Dd]` is closing the match early. The rest (`ependencies:`) gets scoped as a key.

---

## ASSISTANT

The problem: `[^:,}\\]]+` stops at `]`. But `]` can appear inside the regex as part of a character class. TextMate regex can't count brackets, so we need a different terminator. The regex key ends at the `:` that separates key from value -- specifically the `: ` (colon followed by space or `{` or `$` or `[`).

---

## ASSISTANT

The new pattern: `re:` then anything (lazy) until it hits `: ` (colon followed by whitespace) -- which is the key-value separator in the JSON body. That lets `]`, `}`, `:` appear freely inside the regex as long as the key-value colon has a space after it.

TextMate picks up grammar changes on save, no rebuild needed. The highlighting should update immediately.

That's correct behavior. The LSP binary speaks JSON-RPC over stdio -- it expects the VS Code client to send it structured messages, not terminal input. When you type enter, it reads that as invalid JSON-RPC and returns a parse error.

It's not meant to be run directly in a terminal. VS Code launches it as a subprocess and talks to it over stdin/stdout. You'll never interact with it manually.

## ASSISTANT

The parser already tracks position via `pos: usize` in `_1_parse.rs` -- it's the index into the input string. The issue is that `anyhow::bail!` discards it.

Two changes:

1. Custom error type with a `pos` field instead of anyhow:

```rust
#[derive(Debug)]
pub struct ParseError {
    pub pos: usize,
    pub msg: String,
}
```

2. Replace every `anyhow::bail!("msg")` with `return Err(ParseError { pos: *pos, msg: "msg".into() })`.

The parser functions already carry `pos: &mut usize` through every call. The byte offset is right there -- it's just getting thrown away when the error converts to anyhow.

Rough diff: ~30 `bail!` sites become `Err(ParseError { pos, msg })`. The return types change from `anyhow::Result<T>` to `Result<T, ParseError>`. The LSP reads `err.pos` and converts to line/col via `offset_to_position`.

Small change, mechanical. Want to do it now or next session?

## ASSISTANT

Interesting direction. The core idea: `sprf` as a standalone pipe filter, no index required.

```
# like ast-grep --pattern
echo '{"deps":{"lodash":"4.x"}}' | sprf 'json({ deps: { $K: $V } })'

# piped from find/fd, each line is a path -- sprf resolves git context per-line
fd -e json | sprf 'fs(*/package.json) > json({ name: $N }) > pkg($N)'

# git-aware: if a line's resolved path lives in a repo, $REPO/$BRANCH are available
fd -e json | sprf 'repo(sprefa) > json({ name: $N })'
```

Two modes emerge:

1. **Value mode** -- stdin is the content itself (one JSON blob, one file body). Single-stage matcher, no fs/repo/branch slots. Like `jq` but with sprf's destructuring grammar.

2. **Path mode** -- stdin is newline-delimited paths. Each line gets stat'd, optionally git-resolved for repo/branch context, then content-matched. The full selector chain works but without SQLite -- results stream to stdout as TSV or JSON lines.

Anonymous inputs (no path, just piped content) skip the fs/repo/branch slots entirely and jump straight to the content matcher stage. That's clean -- the selector chain just starts at whatever slot the input satisfies.

The runtime is already mostly there in `_3_match.rs`. The missing piece is a thin CLI harness that reads stdin, detects mode (paths vs content), and drives the matcher without touching the DB layer.

Have fun poking at it -- solid foundation to riff on when you're ready to wire it up.

---

## ASSISTANT

**Scan phase**: one `File::open` + mmap per file in `index/src/extract.rs:44`. Rayon-parallelized.

**Watch phase on modify**: `fs::read` in `watcher.rs:568` for re-extraction + diff. Separate open from the scan-phase mmap.

**Watch phase on create**: `File::open` + mmap in `watcher.rs:520` for content hashing only.

So yes, a modified file gets opened twice across the lifecycle -- once during initial scan (mmap'd for extraction), then again on change (read into memory for re-extraction). But these are temporally separated events, not redundant concurrent opens.

The one optimization opportunity: on file create, `hash_file()` opens + mmaps at line 520, but doesn't extract. The extraction happens later when `scan_diff` picks it up -- that's a second open of the same new file. Folding hash + extract into one open on create would eliminate that.

For modify events, the double-open is inherent -- the scan-phase data is long gone from memory by the time the watch event fires, so re-reading is necessary.

## ASSISTANT

**Bulk scan path (flush.rs, resolve.rs)**: properly batched with chunked inserts and `CASE/WHEN` updates. No N+1 there.

**Watch/plan path -- three N+1 hotspots**, all in `crates/watch/src/plan.rs`:

1. **`all_rs_uses_in_repo` called 2-3x for same file_id** (lines 180, 214, 275) -- fetches every Rust ref in the repo, then does it again in the same function. Cache the first result.

2. **`find_chain_root` loop** (line 405-422) -- walks re-export chains one hop per query. A recursive CTE (`WITH RECURSIVE`) would collapse the whole chain into one round-trip.

3. **`rename_chain_step` recursion** (line 446-492) -- queries `import_names_from_file` + `reexport_relay_file_ids` per node, then recurses. Exponential in chain depth. Pre-loading the relay graph and processing iteratively would flatten it.

All three are in the rename/move rewrite planner, not the scan hot path. So initial indexing is clean, but file renames in deep re-export chains (common in barrel-file-heavy TS repos) hit the DB hard.

The file-open double-read + these plan.rs N+1s are the two main runtime inefficiencies. The scan side is solid.

## ASSISTANT

Three fixes, all in `plan.rs`. Here's the lift:

### 1. Cache `all_rs_uses_in_repo` -- trivial
Lines 180, 214, 275 call it for the same `file_id`. Hoist one call, reuse the `Vec`.

```rust
// before the first use at line 179
let all_rs = queries::all_rs_uses_in_repo(pool, file_id).await?;
```

Then replace all three call sites with `&all_rs`. ~5 lines changed, zero risk.

### 2. `find_chain_root` recursive CTE -- small
Replace the loop (lines 405-422) with one query:

```sql
WITH RECURSIVE chain(fid) AS (
    SELECT ?1
    UNION ALL
    SELECT r.source_file_id FROM refs r
    JOIN chain ON r.target_file_id = chain.fid
    WHERE r.kind = 'ImportName' AND r.value = ?2
)
SELECT fid FROM chain ORDER BY rowid DESC LIMIT 1
```

One new query function in `queries.rs`, swap out the loop. ~20 lines. The SQL needs to match whatever `upstream_export_file` does internally -- worth reading that query first.

### 3. Flatten `rename_chain_step` -- medium
The recursion (lines 446-492) is structurally a BFS. Replace with a `VecDeque` work queue:

```rust
let mut queue = VecDeque::from([source_file_id]);
let mut visited = HashSet::new();
while let Some(fid) = queue.pop_front() {
    if !visited.insert(fid) { continue; }
    let affected = queries::import_names_from_file(pool, fid, old_name).await?;
    // ... push edits ...
    let relays = queries::reexport_relay_file_ids(pool, fid, old_name).await?;
    queue.extend(relays);
}
```

Same N+1 per node, but eliminates `Box::pin` recursion overhead and stack depth risk. ~30 lines, replaces ~45. To actually eliminate the per-node queries, you'd pre-load the full relay graph for `old_name` in one query -- that's a second recursive CTE but optional.

**Total lift**: fix 1 is 10 minutes, fix 2 is 30 minutes, fix 3 is 30-45 minutes. All localized to `plan.rs` + one new query.

## ASSISTANT

`cargo check` only runs the frontend (parsing, type checking, borrow checking). No codegen, no linking.

`cargo test` must compile actual binaries -- one per test target -- with codegen and linking. That's the expensive part. For a workspace this size with sqlx/tokio/tree-sitter in the dep tree, the test harness binaries are where the time goes.

The check you just saw was fast because all dependencies were already compiled from the earlier check. The test build starts from scratch for `--test` targets because test binaries are separate compilation units with different cfg flags (`#[cfg(test)]`).

Short version: check validates types, test builds executables. Different artifacts, different cost.

## ASSISTANT

Not documented in the README. Here's what exists:

**Config** (`sprefa.toml`): add a `[ghcache]` section with the path to ghcache's SQLite DB:

```toml
[ghcache]
db = "/path/to/ghcache.db"
poll_ms = 500  # optional, default 500
```

**Sources** filter which repos/branches get scanned when ghcache fires events:

```toml
[[sources]]
# branch_patterns is optional -- omit to accept all branches
branch_patterns = ["main", "feature/*"]
```

**Feature gate**: it's behind `--features ghcache` on the CLI crate. So building with it:

```
cargo build -p sprefa --features ghcache
```

**Runtime**: when `sprefa daemon` starts with `[ghcache]` configured, it subscribes to ghcache's `change_log` table, scans all existing checkouts on startup, then polls every `poll_ms` for new checkout events and rescans incrementally.

None of this is in the README or `--help` currently. Want me to add a section?

## ASSISTANT

Right now there's no way to do that. Every path goes through SQLite:

- `sprefa scan` -> extract -> flush to DB
- `sprefa daemon` -> scan + watch + DB
- Rules are loaded and applied during extraction, results written to `matches` table

No standalone "run this .sprf rule against this file and show me what it captures" command exists.

Two viable shapes:

**1. `sprefa eval` -- single rule, single file, no DB**

```bash
sprefa eval 'json({ dependencies: { $K: $V } })' package.json
# stdout: K=lodash V=^4.17.21
#         K=express V=~4.18.0

sprefa eval -f my-rules.sprf package.json    # all rules in file
echo '{"a":1}' | sprefa eval 'json({ $K: $V })'  # stdin mode
```

This is the pipe filter idea from earlier. The matcher (`_3_match.rs`) already works standalone -- it takes a `Pattern` + `serde_json::Value` and returns captures. No DB needed. For ast-grep patterns, it shells out or calls the ast-grep API directly. For fs() globs, it just filters the input paths.

**2. `sprefa check` -- validate .sprf syntax only**

```bash
sprefa check sprefa-rules.sprf
# sprefa-rules.sprf: 12 rules, 3 links, ok
# or: sprefa-rules.sprf:7:15 error: unclosed json body
```

This just parses and validates without running anything. Useful for CI.

The git context question: repo/branch slots need a git repo to resolve `$REPO`/`$BRANCH`. For a temp dir without git, those slots would just be empty/skipped. The content matchers (json, ast, re) don't care about git at all.

## ASSISTANT

Right. The context stack infers from where you are:

```
sprefa eval <rule> [path...]
```

- **0 paths**: stdin is content (pipe mode)
- **1 path**: that's the fs target, repo/branch from `git rev-parse` at cwd
- **N paths**: each is an fs target, same repo/branch inference

The rule string skips whatever context slots are already satisfied by the environment. If the rule has `repo(foo) > branch(main) > fs(...)`, it checks against the inferred repo/branch. If the rule is just `json({...})`, it jumps straight to content matching on the input files.

```bash
# in a git repo, on branch main
sprefa eval 'json({ dependencies: { $K: $V } })' package.json
# repo = inferred from cwd git root
# branch = main (from HEAD)
# fs = package.json
# -> K=lodash V=^4.17.21

# explicit fs glob, walks the repo
sprefa eval 'fs(**/package.json) > json({ name: $N }) > match($N, pkg)'

# pipe mode, no git context
cat values.yaml | sprefa eval 'json({ image: { repository: $R } })'

# with repo/branch filter in rule -- checks against cwd's git state
sprefa eval 'repo(sprefa) > fs(**/Cargo.toml) > json({ package: { name: $N } })'
```

The matcher stack already exists in `_3_match.rs`. The missing piece is a thin harness that:
1. Resolves git context from cwd (`git rev-parse --show-toplevel`, `git branch --show-current`)
2. Parses the inline rule string
3. If fs() slot present, globs the repo. Otherwise uses positional paths or stdin.
4. Runs the content matcher, prints captures as TSV or JSON lines.

Want me to build it?

## ASSISTANT

Yeah, ambiguity between "is this a branch glob or an fs glob" is real when bare strings are involved. The tagged slots solve it -- if you're using `eval`, require tags:

```bash
# explicit about what you mean
sprefa eval 'fs(**/package.json) > json({ name: $N })'
sprefa eval 'branch(main) > fs(**/*.toml) > json({ package: { name: $N } })'

# content-only, reads stdin
echo '{"a":1}' | sprefa eval 'json({ $K: $V })'
```

No bare glob inference in eval mode. Repo/branch come from cwd's git state automatically (unless overridden with `repo()`/`branch()` slots in the rule). Stdin kicks in when there's no `fs()` slot and no positional path args.

---

## ASSISTANT

That's clean. One bare segment = fs glob, repo/branch from cwd. The moment you need more than one bare segment, use tags.

```bash
# one bare segment -> fs, rest inferred
sprefa eval '**/package.json > json({ name: $N })'
# repo = cwd git root, branch = HEAD

# two bare segments? ambiguous. use tags.
sprefa eval 'branch(release/*) > fs(**/Cargo.toml) > json({ name: $N })'
```

Rule: bare segments in eval are only valid if there's exactly one, and it's treated as fs. Zero bare segments means content-only (stdin or positional path). Two+ bare segments is a parse error with a message like "use fs(), branch(), repo() tags when chaining multiple context slots".

Right. `fs()` is relative to cwd, not repo root. If you're in `crates/cli/`, then `fs(**/Cargo.toml)` only walks under `crates/cli/`. To search the whole repo, `cd` to root or use `../` in the glob.

That matches how `find`, `fd`, `rg` all work -- cwd is the implicit scope.

## ASSISTANT

The workflow you're describing is: an AI greps around, finds "oh, this string in repo A matches that string in repo B because of naming convention X", and then that discovery gets encoded as a .sprf rule so it's reproducible without the AI.

The sequence is:

**1. Discovery** -- AI explores, finds patterns

```
# "what strings appear in multiple repos?"
sprefa sql "SELECT s.value, COUNT(DISTINCT f.repo_id) as repos 
            FROM strings s JOIN refs r ON r.string_id = s.id 
            JOIN files f ON r.file_id = f.id 
            GROUP BY s.value HAVING repos > 1 
            ORDER BY repos DESC LIMIT 50"

# "what kinds of matches exist for this string?"
sprefa sql "SELECT s.value, m.kind, m.rule_name, f.path
            FROM strings s JOIN refs r ON r.string_id = s.id
            JOIN matches m ON m.ref_id = r.id
            JOIN files f ON r.file_id = f.id
            WHERE s.value = 'user-service'"
```

**2. Pattern recognition** -- AI sees the shape

```
# "every values.yaml has image.repository that matches a package.json name 
#  after stripping the org prefix"
# that's a link rule with norm_eq + a strip_suffixes config
```

**3. Encoding** -- write the .sprf rule

```sprf
fs(**/values.yaml) > json({ **: { image: { repository: $REPO } } })
  > match($REPO, image_repo);
link(image_repo > package_name, norm_eq) > $image_source;
```

**4. Validation** -- eval confirms it

```bash
sprefa eval 'fs(**/values.yaml) > json({ **: { image: { repository: $R } } })' 
```

The missing piece is step 1-2 at scale. Right now the AI has to manually query the DB. What would close the loop:

**`sprefa discover`** -- given two match kinds, find what predicate connects them:

```bash
sprefa discover image_repo package_name
# output:
#   norm_eq: 47 matches (strip "org/" prefix)
#   string_eq: 12 matches (exact)
#   no match: 3 (image_repo="legacy-svc" has no package_name peer)
```

**`sprefa suggest`** -- given a file pattern, propose extraction rules by sampling content:

```bash
sprefa suggest '**/values.yaml'
# output:
#   found 23 files
#   common keys at depth 2+: image.repository (21 files), image.tag (21 files)
#   image.repository values look like package names (norm_eq to package_name: 89%)
#   suggested rule:
#     fs(**/values.yaml) > json({ **: { image: { repository: $REPO, tag: $TAG } } })
#       > match($REPO, image_repo) > match($TAG, image_tag);
```

**`sprefa trace`** -- record an AI's grep session as a replayable .sprf rule:

```bash
sprefa trace start
# AI does: grep -r "user-service" across repos
# AI does: finds it in package.json name, k8s deployment, deploy values
# AI does: realizes the link is norm_eq after stripping -service suffix
sprefa trace stop
# output: here's the .sprf rules that encode what you just found
```

The trace idea is the most interesting -- it turns the AI's exploratory session into declarative rules. The AI's greps become `fs()` + `json()` selectors, and the cross-repo connections it found become `link()` declarations.

Which of these directions feels most useful to build next?

---

## ASSISTANT

This is the piece. The `node_path` field on `RawRef` already stores the structural path through the parsed tree. The anti-unification algorithm takes two refs' full paths and computes the .sprf rule that would extract both.

The connection to `sprefa eval` and the "zero to 100%" question:

```
1. AI greps around, finds two things that mean the same thing
2. sprefa anti-unify <ref_id_1> <ref_id_2>  ->  .sprf rule
3. sprefa eval '<generated rule>'            ->  verify it catches all instances
4. append to sprefa-rules.sprf
5. sprefa scan picks it up, now indexed permanently
```

The `node_path` is already being stored during extraction (`emit.rs` builds it from `result.path`). The `lgg` algorithm in the doc is ~50 lines of actual logic. The segment equality table maps directly to .sprf slot types:

| lgg segment | .sprf output |
|---|---|
| repo differs | `repo(*)` or omit |
| branch differs | `branch(*)` or omit |
| rel_path same extension | `fs(*.ext)` |
| rel_path same filename | `fs(**/filename)` |
| yaml key same | `json({ key: ... })` |
| yaml key differs | `json({ $K: ... })` |
| value differs but typed | `$VERSION`, `$REPO_NAME` etc |

So `sprefa anti-unify` (or `sprefa learn`) would:

1. Take two ref IDs or two match IDs from the index
2. Load their full paths from the DB (the SQL is already in the doc)
3. Run `lgg` segment by segment
4. Emit a .sprf rule string

The eval command you just built is the verification step. The anti-unify command is the generation step. Together they close the loop in the doc's diagram.

What's the lift: the `lgg` function itself is small. The segment comparison table is the bulk of the logic. Output formatting to .sprf syntax is mechanical given the AST types already exist. Probably ~200 lines in a new file, plus the CLI command wiring.

Want to build it?

---

## ASSISTANT

No. Right now captures on context slots grab the *whole value*, not what the glob matched. `branch(release/*) > capture($B)` would give `$B = "release/v3"` -- the entire branch name, not `"v3"`.

There's no glob segment capture. The glob is used for *filtering* (does this branch match `release/*`?), then the capture just grabs `ctx.branch` wholesale.

Three levels of what "capture from a glob" could mean:

**1. Current: capture the whole value** (works now)
```sprf
# $B = "release/v3.2"
branch(release/*) > ... > match($B, branch_name)
```

**2. Named glob segments** (doesn't exist)
```sprf
# $VER = "v3.2"  
branch(release/$VER) > ... > match($VER, release_version)

# $ORG = "acme", $REPO = "frontend"
repo($ORG/$REPO) > ...

# $DIR = "packages", $NAME = "widgets"
fs($DIR/$NAME/package.json) > ...
```

**3. Glob with regex fallback for complex extraction**
```sprf
# capture from a more complex pattern
branch(re:release/(?P<MAJOR>\d+)\.(?P<MINOR>\d+)) > ...
```

Level 2 is the interesting one. It would require the glob matcher to report *which segments* of the input matched which parts of the pattern, not just pass/fail. `globset` doesn't do that -- it's a boolean matcher.

Implementation options for level 2:
- Split the glob pattern on `/`, split the input on `/`, zip and match segment by segment. `$VAR` segments capture, literal segments must match exactly, `*` segments match one segment, `**` matches N segments.
- This is basically a mini path-pattern matcher, ~40 lines. The glob crate isn't needed -- it's simple enough to do directly since path segments are `/`-delimited.

That would make anti-unification output much tighter rules. Instead of `repo(*) > branch(*)` with the whole value captured, you'd get `repo($ORG/$REPO) > branch(release/$VER)` with structured captures.

## ASSISTANT

Branch and tag are separate slots. `branch()` matches branch names, `tag()` matches git tags. Both exist in the AST already.

On the capture question -- what you're describing:

```sprf
repo($ORG/$REPO)              # $ORG=one segment, $REPO=one segment
branch(release/$$$REST)        # $$$REST=multiple segments  
fs(packages/$NAME/src/$$$PATH) # $NAME=one, $$$PATH=rest
```

With implicit parent = full match:

```
input: "acme/frontend"
pattern: $ORG/$REPO
captures: ORG="acme", REPO="frontend"
parent of both: "acme/frontend"
```

The parent part isn't hard conceptually -- every capture already comes from a context slot that has the full value (`ctx.repo`, `ctx.branch`, `path`). The full match is just the value before splitting.

The segment matching itself isn't hard either. Split pattern and input on `/`, walk in lockstep:

```
pattern segments: [$ORG, $REPO]
input segments:   [acme, frontend]
-> ORG=acme, REPO=frontend
```

`$$$` is the tricky one. It's greedy over N segments, same problem as `**` in glob. With one `$$$` per pattern it's unambiguous (it eats everything not claimed by surrounding literals/singles). With two `$$$` it's ambiguous. Restrict to one `$$$` per pattern and it's ~30 lines.

```rust
fn match_segments(pattern: &[Segment], input: &[&str]) -> Option<Vec<(String, String)>> {
    // walk both, $VAR eats 1, $$$VAR eats N (greedy from right), literal must match
}

enum Segment {
    Literal(String),
    Capture(String),      // $X -- one segment
    MultiCapture(String), // $$$X -- N segments, joined with /
    Wild,                 // $_ -- one segment, no bind
    MultiWild,            // $$$_ -- N segments, no bind (same as **)
}
```

The hard part is: where does this live? Right now `CompiledGitSelector` and `CompiledFileSelector` use `globset` which is boolean. You'd replace those with this segment matcher for patterns that contain `$` captures, keep globset as fallback for plain globs.

Not hard. Maybe 80 lines for the segment matcher + 30 lines to wire it into compile_rule. The parent is free -- it's just the original `ctx.branch` / `ctx.repo` / `path` value that was already available.

branch(re:release/(?P<MAJOR>\d+)\.(?P<MINOR>\d+))
fs(re:packages/(?P<NAME>[^/]+)/src/.+\.ts)

| Prefix | Engine | Captures

## ASSISTANT

Good. `re:` already works everywhere in `git_match.rs` -- glob or regex, `is_match` is boolean. The `PatternMatcher` enum already has both arms. But captures aren't extracted from either.

On your questions:

**Merging tag + branch into `ref()`**: In git, branches and tags are both refs under `refs/heads/` and `refs/tags/`. They share the same namespace rules. Merging them makes sense -- a git ref is a git ref. The distinction only matters for the DB schema (`branch_name` vs `git_tag` in `repo_refs`), not for pattern matching.

```sprf
# current: two separate slots
branch(release/*) > tag(v*) > fs(...)

# merged: ref() matches either, prefix disambiguates if needed
ref(release/*)     # matches branches named release/*
ref(tags/v*)       # matches tags (if you need to distinguish)
ref(*)             # matches any ref
```

Though honestly, for most use cases people know whether they mean a branch or a tag. Keeping them separate in the DSL but backed by the same matching engine is probably cleaner than forcing `ref()` everywhere.

**Regex capture groups must be screaming**: Yes, consistent with everything else. `(?P<VERSION>\d+\.\d+)` not `(?P<version>...)`. The parser can enforce this -- reject non-screaming named groups at parse time.

**Current state of captures in pattern positions**:

`PatternMatcher::is_match()` returns `bool`. No captures extracted. To add captures:

```rust
enum PatternMatcher {
    Glob(GlobMatcher),
    Regex(Regex),
    SegmentCapture(Vec<Segment>),  // new: $ORG/$REPO style
}

impl PatternMatcher {
    // bool match (existing, for filtering)
    fn is_match(&self, value: &str) -> bool { ... }
    
    // capturing match (new, for extraction)
    fn captures(&self, value: &str) -> Option<Vec<(String, String)>> {
        match self {
            Self::Regex(r) => {
                let caps = r.captures(value)?;
                Some(r.capture_names()
                    .flatten()
                    .filter_map(|name| Some((name.to_string(), caps.name(name)?.as_str().to_string())))
                    .collect())
            }
            Self::SegmentCapture(segments) => {
                match_segments(segments, value)
            }
            Self::Glob(_) => None, // plain globs don't capture
        }
    }
}
```

Then `resolve_context_captures` goes from "grab the whole value" to "if the pattern has captures, use those; otherwise fall back to whole value."

The unified picture:

| Pattern | Engine | Captures |
|---|---|---|
| `release/*` | glob | none (whole value via `$B` on the slot) |
| `$ORG/$REPO` | segment | `ORG`, `REPO` from path split |
| `re:release/(?P<VER>.+)` | regex | `VER` from named group |
| `*` or `**` | glob | none |
| `$NAME` | segment | `NAME` (one segment) |
| `$$$REST` | segment | `REST` (multi segment, joined with `/`) |

All three engines coexist in the same `PatternMatcher` enum. Detection is: starts with `re:` -> regex, contains `$` -> segment capture, otherwise -> glob.

```sprf
# this means AND -- branch must match AND tag must match
branch(release/*) > tag(v*) > fs(...)

## ASSISTANT

Yes. Separate slots are AND in the current chain semantics. There's no way to say "match if branch is release/* OR tag is v*" with `branch()` and `tag()` as distinct slots.

`ref()` solves it because branches and tags are both refs -- the OR is implicit in the matching:

```sprf
ref(release/*|v*)  # matches branch release/foo OR tag v1.0
```

And since `git_match.rs` already handles pipe-delimited alternatives within a single pattern, `ref()` gets OR for free from existing code. Just needs one `ref` field in `CompiledGitSelector` that checks against both `ctx.branch` and `ctx.tags`.
